key_insight: Physics-informed residual neural networks can model hot deformation and dynamic recrystallization in titanium alloys more accurately and more physically consistently than conventional constitutive fits.
engineering_relevance: This enables better prediction of flow stress during thermomechanical processing, supporting improved process design for advanced Ti alloys.
method: Built two stacked residual physics-informed neural networks (STAR-PINNs) using a shared residual encoder.
Enforced constitutive physics with automatic differentiation: strain hardening, strain-rate sensitivity, thermal softening, and post-peak softening.
Added a DRX-aware dual-output design with JMAK-Avrami regularization, DRX saturation constraints, and stress–DRX latent-feature fusion.
evidence_signal: Trained on experimental flow-stress data from 800–1050 °C and 0.01–10 s^-1, and the DRX-aware model achieved RMSE 11.69 MPa, MAE 4.83 MPa, R^2 = 0.9850, with cross-validated RMSE 12.47 ± 0.26 MPa.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and coverage of experimental hot-compression data, so extrapolation outside the tested temperature/strain-rate range may be unreliable.
paper_title: Physics-Informed Residual Deep Learning for Constitutive Modeling of Hot Deformation and Dynamic Recrystallization in a Mo-Rich $α+β$ Titanium Alloy
